content: The bond angles in the following molecules decreases in the order. $\mathrm{BF}_3, \mathrm{NH}_3, \mathrm{PF}_3$ and $\mathrm{XeF}_2$
explanation:
We can rank these by their typical bond angles:

• XeF₂ (linear):

F–Xe–F = $$180°$$

• BF₃ (trigonal planar):

F–B–F = $$120°$$

• NH₃ (trigonal pyramidal):

H–N–H ≈ $$107.5°$$

• PF₃ (trigonal pyramidal):

F–P–F ≈ $$98°$$

Putting them in decreasing order gives:

$$\text{XeF}_2\;(180°) > \text{BF}_3\;(120°) > \text{NH}_3\;(107.5°) > \text{PF}_3\;(98°)$$

That matches Option B.

content:
Arrange the following in the increasing order of their covalent character.

$\mathrm{CaF}_2 ; \mathrm{CaCl}_2 ; \mathrm{CaBr}_2 ; \mathrm{CaI}_2$
explanation:
According to Fajans’ rules, the covalent character of an ionic compound increases when the anion is more easily polarizable. Keeping Ca²⁺ fixed, the halide polarizabilities increase down the group:

F⁻ (smallest, least polarizable)

Cl⁻

Br⁻

I⁻ (largest, most polarizable)

Hence the covalent character order (least → most) is:

$$\mathrm{CaF}_2 \;<\; \mathrm{CaCl}_2 \;<\; \mathrm{CaBr}_2 \;<\; \mathrm{CaI}_2$$

This corresponds to option D.

content: Given are 4 pairs of covalent molecules. Identify the pair in which both molecules have the same shape.
explanation:
The only pair that share the same molecular shape is Option A.

XeF₂

– Electron‐pair geometry: trigonal–bipyramidal

– Lone pairs: 3 (all equatorial)

– Molecular shape: linear

HgCl₂

– Electron‐pair geometry: linear (no lone pairs on central Hg)

– Molecular shape: linear

All the others differ (BF₃ is trigonal‐planar vs NH₃ pyramidal; XeF₄ square‐planar vs SF₄ seesaw; BrF₅ square‐pyramidal vs PCl₅ trigonal‐bipyramidal). Therefore, Option A is correct.

content:
From among the 4 given compounds identify the compounds which possess a net dipole moment.

A. cis-1,2-Dichloroethene

B. Tetrachloromethane

C. o-Dichlorobenzene

D. trans 2,3-Dibromobut-2-ene
explanation:
The only polar molecules are cis-1,2-dichloroethene (A) and o-dichlorobenzene (C). Hence the correct choice is Option C (A and C).

• cis-1,2-Dichloroethene (A)

– Two C–Cl bond dipoles point roughly in the same direction, so they don’t cancel.

– Net dipole $$\vec\mu_{\rm net}=\vec\mu_{C–Cl_1}+\vec\mu_{C–Cl_2}\neq0.$$

• Tetrachloromethane (B)

– Tetrahedral symmetry makes all C–Cl dipoles cancel (non-polar).

• o-Dichlorobenzene (C)

– The two adjacent C–Cl dipoles are separated by 60°, so their vector sum is nonzero.

• trans-2,3-Dibromobut-2-ene (D)

– Two C–Br dipoles are exactly opposite (180°), so they cancel (non-polar).

content: Which one of the following molecules attains greater stability on formation of its diatomic monovalent anion?
explanation:
Among these, only C₂ gains a bond‐strength (and thus a stability) on adding one electron. Here’s why, in terms of MO bond orders:

C₂ (8 e⁻)

– MO config: σ2s² σ*2s² π2pₓ² π2pᵧ²

– Bond order = (6 bonding – 2 antibonding)/2 = 2

– C₂⁻ (9 e⁻): the 9th e⁻ goes into σ2p_z (a bonding orbital)

– New bond order = (7 – 2)/2 = 2.5 → bond order ↑, more stable

N₂ (10 e⁻) → N₂⁻ (11 e⁻) adds into π*2p (antibonding) → bond order drops 3 → 2.5

O₂ (12 e⁻) → O₂⁻ (13 e⁻) adds into π*2p → bond order drops 2 → 1.5

F₂ (14 e⁻) → F₂⁻ (15 e⁻) adds into π*2p → bond order drops 1 → 0.5

Only in C₂ does the extra electron enter a bonding orbital.

Answer: Option A, C₂.

content: In which one of the following pairs does the stability of the monovalent ion increase with respect to the molecule, while the magnetic character remains the same for both?
explanation:
Option B, O₂ ⟶ O₂⁺

Explanation via MO theory:

O₂ electronic configuration (valence MOs):

$$\sigma_{2s}^2,\;\sigma_{2s}^*{}^2,\;\sigma_{2p_z}^2,\;\pi_{2p_x}^2,\;\pi_{2p_y}^2,\;\pi_{2p_x}^*{}^1,\;\pi_{2p_y}^*{}^1$$

– Bonding electrons = 2 (σ₂s) + 2 (σ₂p_z) + 4 (π₂p) = 8

– Antibonding electrons = 2 (σ₂s) + 2 (π₂p) = 4

– Bond order = (8 – 4)/2 = 2

– Unpaired electrons in π* ⇒ paramagnetic

O₂⁺ removes one electron from the highest MO (a π* orbital):

– Bonding electrons remain 8

– Antibonding electrons = 2 (σ₂s) + 1 (π) = 3

– Bond order = (8 – 3)/2 = 2.5 (increased stability)

– Still one unpaired electron ⇒ remains paramagnetic

No other diatomic in the list both increases bond order on ionization and keeps the same magnetic character.

content: Identify the correct statement.

options.content: The central atoms in both $\mathrm{CH}_4$ and $\mathrm{SF}_4$ are in a state of $\mathrm{sp}^3$ hybridisation
The resultant dipole moment of $\mathrm{NF}_3$ is greater than that of $\mathrm{NH}_3$
The $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{O}$ bond length in $\mathrm{CO}_2$ molecule is 110 pm because of the Inductive effect $(+\mathrm{I})$ of oxygen
A molecule of the type $\mathrm{AB}_5 \mathrm{E}$ where the central atom has 5 bond pairs and 1 lone pair has a square pyramid geometry

explanation:
The correct statement is Option D:

• In an AB₅E species (5 bonding pairs + 1 lone pair), VSEPR predicts a square-pyramidal shape.

– The lone pair occupies one of the axial positions in an octahedral arrangement, leaving a square base of five ligands.

All other options are false:

A. SF₄ is sp³d (see-saw), not sp³.

B. μ(NF₃) ≈ 0.24 D < μ(NH₃) ≈ 1.47 D.

C. C=O in CO₂ is ≈116 pm (double bond), and oxygen exerts a –I effect, not +I.

content:
From the given covalent compounds (A to F) identify the pair of molecules which have:

(i) Two lone pairs of electrons on the central atom.

(ii) One lone pair of electrons on the central atom.

A. $\mathrm{SO}_2$
B. $\mathrm{ClF}_3$
C. $\mathrm{BF}_3$
D. $\mathrm{BrF}_5$
E. XeF 4
F. $\mathrm{SF}_6$
explanation:
Let’s count the valence electrons on each central atom, subtract the ones used in bonds, and see how many remain as lone‐pair electrons:

SO₂ (A)

• S has 6 valence e⁻

• It forms two double bonds → uses 4 e⁻

• Remaining 2 e⁻ ⇒ 1 lone pair

ClF₃ (B)

• Cl has 7 valence e⁻

• It forms three single bonds → uses 3 e⁻

• Remaining 4 e⁻ ⇒ 2 lone pairs

BF₃ (C)

• B has 3 valence e⁻

• It forms three single bonds → uses 3 e⁻

• Remaining 0 e⁻ ⇒ 0 lone pairs

BrF₅ (D)

• Br has 7 valence e⁻

• It forms five single bonds → uses 5 e⁻

• Remaining 2 e⁻ ⇒ 1 lone pair

XeF₄ (E)

• Xe has 8 valence e⁻

• It forms four single bonds → uses 4 e⁻

• Remaining 4 e⁻ ⇒ 2 lone pairs

SF₆ (F)

• S has 6 valence e⁻

• It forms six single bonds → uses 6 e⁻

• Remaining 0 e⁻ ⇒ 0 lone pairs

Hence:

(i) Two lone pairs on the central atom → B (ClF₃) & E (XeF₄)

(ii) One lone pair on the central atom → A (SO₂) & D (BrF₅)

That corresponds to Option C.

content: Identify the pair of molecules in which one of them is a molecule with an odd electron and the other has an expanded octet.
explanation:
To determine which of the given pairs of molecules contains one molecule with an odd electron and another with an expanded octet, we need to analyze the electron configurations and bonding structures of each pair:

Option A:

$$\mathrm{BeCl}_2$$ & $$\mathrm{HNO}_3$$

  • $$\mathrm{BeCl}_2$$: Beryllium chloride has a linear structure and does not have an odd electron.

  • $$\mathrm{HNO}_3$$: Nitric acid does not have an odd electron nor an expanded octet. The nitrogen in $$\mathrm{HNO}_3$$ obeys the octet rule.

Option B:

$$\mathrm{NO}$$ & $$\mathrm{PF}_5$$

  • $$\mathrm{NO}$$: Nitric oxide has an odd electron (one unpaired electron).

  • $$\mathrm{PF}_5$$: Phosphorus pentafluoride has an expanded octet (10 electrons around phosphorus).

Option C:

$$\mathrm{BCl}_3$$ & $$\mathrm{NO}_2$$

  • $$\mathrm{BCl}_3$$: Boron trichloride does not have an odd electron and boron typically does not have an expanded octet.

  • $$\mathrm{NO}_2$$: Nitrogen dioxide has an odd electron but does not have an expanded octet.

Option D:

$$\mathrm{SCl}_2$$ & $$\mathrm{NH}_3$$

  • $$\mathrm{SCl}_2$$: Sulfur dichloride has no odd electron and typically does not have an expanded octet.

  • $$\mathrm{NH}_3$$: Ammonia has no odd electron and does not exhibit an expanded octet.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option B: $$\mathrm{NO}$$ & $$\mathrm{PF}_5$$

content:
Which of the following two molecular species are Diamagnetic in nature?

$$[\mathrm{A}]=\mathrm{O}_2^{-} \quad[\mathrm{B}] .=\mathrm{N}_2^{+} \quad[\mathrm{C}]=\mathrm{C}_2 \quad[\mathrm{D}]=\mathrm{O}_2{ }^{2-}$$

Choose the incorrect statement from the following:

A. Isoelectronic molecules/ions have the same bond order.

B. Dipole moment of $$\mathrm{NH}_3$$ is greater than that of $$\mathrm{NF}_3$$.

C. The Carbon in Methyl Carbocation is $$\mathrm{sp}^3$$ hybridised.

D. The stability of an ionic compound is measured in terms of its lattice enthalpy and not simply based on attaining Octet configuration.
explanation:
Let's analyze each statement one by one to determine which is incorrect:

Statement A: Isoelectronic molecules/ions have the same bond order.

This statement is incorrect. While isoelectronic species have the same number of electrons, they can have different bond orders due to differences in their nuclear charges and arrangement of electrons in molecular orbitals. Therefore, isoelectronic molecules/ions do not necessarily have the same bond order.

Statement B: Dipole moment of $$\mathrm{NH}_3$$ is greater than that of $$\mathrm{NF}_3$$.

This statement is correct. In $$\mathrm{NH}_3$$, the lone pair on the nitrogen contributes to a higher dipole moment because the dipole moments of the N-H bonds point towards the nitrogen, reinforcing the overall dipole moment. In $$\mathrm{NF}_3$$, the highly electronegative fluorine atoms pull electron density away, reducing the overall dipole moment of the molecule.

Statement C: The Carbon in Methyl Carbocation is $$\mathrm{sp}^3$$ hybridised.

This statement is incorrect. The carbon in a methyl carbocation ($$\mathrm{CH}_3^+$$) is $$\mathrm{sp}^2$$ hybridised because it has only three regions of electron density (three bonds and no lone pairs), forming a planar structure.

Statement D: The stability of an ionic compound is measured in terms of its lattice enthalpy and not simply based on attaining Octet configuration.

This statement is correct. The stability of an ionic compound is indeed largely determined by its lattice enthalpy, which is the energy released when ions come together to form a lattice structure. Attaining an octet configuration alone does not fully account for the stability without considering lattice enthalpy.

Given this analysis, the incorrect statements are A and C. However, since we must choose one option, we should select the first incorrect statement from the provided options.

Choice A: Isoelectronic molecules/ions have the same bond order.

The correct answer is Option A.

content: $$ \text { From the following pair of compounds, identify the incorrect pair in terms of the covalent character. } $$

options.content:
$$
A g C l>A g I
$$

explanation:
Let's analyze each option using Fajans' rules, which tell us that covalent character in ionic compounds increases with:

• A smaller, highly charged cation (which has a strong polarizing effect)

• A larger, more polarizable anion

Now, let’s examine each option:

$$\textbf{Option A: } \mathrm{CuS} > \mathrm{CuO}$$

Both compounds have the same copper cation.

The sulfide ion ($$S^{2-}$$) is larger and more polarizable than the oxide ion ($$O^{2-}$$).

Thus, $$\mathrm{CuS}$$ should indeed be more covalent than $$\mathrm{CuO}$$.

$$\textbf{Option B: } \mathrm{BeCl}_2 > \mathrm{MgCl}_2$$

Both compounds contain a $$+2$$ cation and a chloride anion.

Beryllium ($$Be^{2+}$$) is much smaller than magnesium ($$Mg^{2+}$$), which makes $$Be^{2+}$$ a more powerful polarizer.

Hence, $$\mathrm{BeCl}_2$$ is expected to have more covalent character than $$\mathrm{MgCl}_2$$.

$$\textbf{Option C: } \mathrm{SnCl}_4 > \mathrm{SnCl}_2$$

Here, tin is in two different oxidation states: $$+4$$ in $$\mathrm{SnCl}_4$$ and $$+2$$ in $$\mathrm{SnCl}_2$$.

A higher oxidation state (i.e., $$+4$$) enhances the cation’s polarizing ability, leading to a more covalent bond.

Therefore, $$\mathrm{SnCl}_4$$ indeed has more covalent character than $$\mathrm{SnCl}_2$$.

$$\textbf{Option D: } \mathrm{AgCl} > \mathrm{AgI}$$

In both compounds, the cation ($$\mathrm{Ag^+}$$) is the same.

However, when comparing the halide anions, iodine ($$\mathrm{I^-}$$) is larger and more polarizable than chloride ($$\mathrm{Cl^-}$$).

According to Fajans' rules, a larger, more polarizable ion like $$\mathrm{I^-}$$ should confer a higher covalent character. Thus, the correct order should be:

$$\mathrm{AgI} > \mathrm{AgCl}$$

Option D incorrectly states that $$\mathrm{AgCl}$$ is more covalent than $$\mathrm{AgI}$$.

Based on this analysis, the incorrect pair in terms of the covalent character is:

$$\boxed{\text{Option D: } \mathrm{AgCl} > \mathrm{AgI}}$$

content: The bond dissociation enthalpy of the species in their correct order is:
explanation:
The key to solving this problem is to understand the concept of bond order, which is the net difference between electrons in bonding and antibonding molecular orbitals divided by 2. A higher bond order typically means a stronger bond and a higher bond dissociation enthalpy.

Here's how the bond orders work out for each species:

$$\mathbf{O_2}$$ (Neutral oxygen):

Contains 16 electrons with a bond order of 2.

$$\mathbf{O_2^+}$$ (Cation):

Removing one electron (preferably from an antibonding orbital) increases the bond order to 2.5.

$$\mathbf{O_2^-}$$ (Superoxide ion):

Adding one electron decreases the bond order to 1.5.

$$\mathbf{O_2^{2-}}$$ (Peroxide ion):

Adding two electrons (populating antibonding orbitals further) decreases the bond order to 1.

Because bond dissociation enthalpy increases with bond order, the species with the highest bond order will have the strongest bond.

Therefore, the order is:

$$\mathrm{O}_2^{+}\, (bond\: order\: 2.5) > \mathrm{O}_2\, (bond\: order\: 2) > \mathrm{O}_2^{-}\, (bond\: order\: 1.5) > \mathrm{O}_2^{2-}\, (bond\: order\: 1)$$

This corresponds to:

Option C:

$$\mathrm{O}_2^{+}>\mathrm{O}_2>\mathrm{O}_2^{-}>\mathrm{O}_2^{2-}$$

content:
In the following question a statement of Assertion (A) followed by a statement of Reason (R) is given. Choose the correct option out of the choices given below.

Assertion (A): A molecule of $$\mathrm{SF}_4$$ is see-saw shaped, while that of $$\mathrm{CIF}_3$$ is T-shaped.

Reason(R): SF$$_4$$ has two lone pair of electrons. But CIF$$_3$$ has one pair of electrons.

options.content: Assertion (A) is incorrect but Reason (R) is correct
Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are incorrect
Assertion (A) is correct but Reason (R) is incorrect

explanation:
Let's analyze the statements step by step:

For $$\mathrm{SF}_4$$:

Sulfur has six valence electrons.

In $$\mathrm{SF}_4$$, sulfur forms four bonds with fluorine atoms. This gives a total of 5 electron domains (4 bonding pairs + 1 lone pair).

With a trigonal bipyramidal electron geometry, the presence of one lone pair results in a see-saw molecular shape.

Thus, the part of the assertion stating that $$\mathrm{SF}_4$$ is see-saw shaped is correct.

For $$\mathrm{ClF}_3$$:

Chlorine has seven valence electrons.

In $$\mathrm{ClF}_3$$, chlorine forms bonds with three fluorine atoms. This gives 5 electron domains (3 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs).

With two lone pairs in a trigonal bipyramidal arrangement, the resultant molecular shape is T-shaped.

Thus, the assertion that $$\mathrm{ClF}_3$$ is T-shaped is also correct.

Now, turning to the Reason (R):

The reason incorrectly states that $$\mathrm{SF}_4$$ has two lone pairs (it actually has one), and that $$\mathrm{ClF}_3$$ has one pair of electrons (it actually has two lone pairs).

Because the assertion is correct but the reason is wrong, the correct choice is:

Option D: Assertion (A) is correct but Reason (R) is incorrect.

content:
On the basis of VSEPR theory, match the molecules listed in Column I with their shapes given in Column II.

No. Column I No. Column II
A $$\mathrm{ClF_3}$$ P Sea saw
B $$\mathrm{BrF_5}$$ Q Pentagonal bipyramidal
C $$\mathrm{SF_4}$$ R Square pyramidal
D $$\mathrm{IF_7}$$ S T-shaped
Identify the pair of molecules, both of which have positive values of Dipole moment.

Given below are 4 molecular species/ions. Identify the species/ion which exhibits diamagnetic nature.

$$[\mathrm{A}]=\mathrm{N}_2^{+} \quad[\mathrm{B}]=\mathrm{O}_2^{-} \quad[\mathrm{C}]=\mathrm{B}_2 \quad[\mathrm{D}]=\mathrm{C}_2$$
Which of these represents the correct order of decreasing bond order?
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { } \mathrm{He}_2^{+}: \sigma 1 s^2, \sigma 1 s^1 \\ & \mathrm{BO}=\frac{1}{2}\left(N_b-N_a\right)=\frac{1}{2}(2-1)=\frac{1}{2}=0.5 \\ & \mathrm{C}_2^{2-}: \sigma 1 s^2, \sigma^* 1 s^2, \sigma 2 s^2, \sigma^* 2 s^2,\left(\pi 2 p_x^2 \approx \pi 2 p_y^2\right), \sigma 2 p_z^2 \\ & \mathrm{BO}=\frac{1}{2}(10-4)=\frac{6}{2}=3.0 \\ & \mathrm{O}_2^{+}=\sigma 1 s^2, \sigma^* 1 s^2, \sigma 2 s^2, \sigma^* 2 s^2 \sigma 2 p_z^2\left(\pi 2 p_x^2 \approx \pi 2 p_y^2\right), \\ & \pi^* 2 p_x^1 \\ & \mathrm{O}_2^{-}=\frac{1}{2}(10-5)=\frac{5}{2}=2.5 \\ & \mathrm{BO}=\frac{1}{2}(10-7)=\frac{3}{2}=1.5 \end{aligned}$$

Thus, the correct order of decreasing bond order is

$$\mathrm{C}_2^{2-}>\mathrm{O}_2^{+}>\mathrm{O}_2^{-}>\mathrm{He}_2^{+}$$

explanation:

As, nitrogen is more electronegative than hydrogen, the resultant dipole moment adds up in the same directioin.

Dipole moment is a vector quantity.

Which of these molecules have non-bonding electron pairs on the cental atom?

content: Identify the correct statement describing the characteristics of $$\mathrm{C}_2$$ molecule.

options.content: Bond order $$=2.0 ; \quad$$ Paramagnetic in nature; One sigma and one pi bond formed.
Bond order $$=2.0 ; \quad$$ Diamagnetic in nature; Both bonds formed are pi bonds.
Bond order $$=1.5 ; \quad$$ Paramagnetic in nature; Both bonds formed are sigma bonds $$(\sigma)$$.

explanation:
The correct identification of the characteristics of the $$\mathrm{C}_2$$ molecule can be established by analyzing the electronic configuration and bond formation of the carbon atoms involved. Each carbon in $$\mathrm{C}_2$$ contributes two electrons to the molecular orbitals.

To evaluate the available options:

  • Option A: Suggests a bond order of 2, stating the molecule is paramagnetic with one sigma and one pi bond.
  • Option B: Claims a bond order of 0 and deems $$\mathrm{C}_2$$ non-existent.
  • Option C: Indicates a bond order of 2, labeling it as diamagnetic with both bonds being pi bonds.
  • Option D: Proposes a bond order of 1.5 with paramagnetic nature; states both bonds formed are sigma bonds.

The bonding in dioxygen can be analyzed via Molecular Orbital Theory (MOT). In the molecular orbital configuration of $$\mathrm{C}_2$$, which comprises 2 carbon atoms each having 2 electrons in the valence p orbital (a total of 4 electrons), the molecular orbital filling is as follows:

Firstly, electrons fill the lowest energy $$\sigma_{2s}$$, followed by $$\sigma_{2s}^*$$, $$\pi_{2p_x}$$, and $$\pi_{2p_y}$$ (which are degenerate) and finally $$\sigma_{2p_z}$$. Each of these π orbitals (from $$\pi_{2p_x}$$ and $$\pi_{2p_y}$$) would ideally accommodate 2 electrons, one from each carbon atom.

The bond order is calculated by the formula:

$$\text{Bond Order} = \frac{\text{Number of electrons in bonding orbitals} - \text{Number of electrons in antibonding orbitals}}{2}$$

In $$\mathrm{C}_2$$, assuming each carbon atom initially has one electron in each $$\pi_{2p_x}$$ and one in $$\pi_{2p_y}$$ with none in the $$\sigma_{2p_z}$$ due to higher energy level at initial stage,

$$\text{Bond Order} = \frac{(4 \text{ (in } \pi_{2p_x} \text{ and } \pi_{2p_y} \text{ orbitals))} - (0)}{2} = 2.$$

This indicates two pi bonds are formed and no sigma bond.

Furthermore, the presence of unpaired electrons in the molecular orbitals renders the molecule paramagnetic.

Analysis: Although Option A correctly states the bond order and the paramagnetic nature, it incorrectly assigns the bond types (stating one sigma and one pi bond). Conversely, Option C precisely asserts the two pi bonds but incorrectly labels the molecule as diamagnetic. Thus, the closest correct option, despite the error in magnetic character, is:

Option C: Bond order $$=2.0 ; \quad$$ Diamagnetic in nature; Both bonds formed are pi bonds.

It accurately comments on the bond order and the configurations but errs in the magnetic property; $$\mathrm{C}_2$$ is indeed paramagnetic, not diamagnetic as mentioned in Option C.

content:
In the following question, Assertion (A) is given followed by a statement of Reason (R). Choose the correct answer.

Assertion (A): $$\pi\left(2 p_x\right), \pi\left(2 p_y\right), \pi^*\left(2 p_x\right), \pi^*\left(2 p_y\right)$$ molecular orbitals have one nodal plane each.

Reason $$(\mathrm{R})$$ : All the molecular orbitals formed by the sideways overlapping have one nodal plane.

options.content: Both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
Both assertion and reason are false
Assertion is true but reason is false
Both assertion and reason are true and reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion.

explanation:
To answer this question, let's first analyze the Assertion (A) and the Reason (R) separately and then relate them.

Analysis of Assertion (A):

The assertion states that the molecular orbitals $$\pi\left(2 p_x\right), \pi\left(2 p_y\right), \pi^*\left(2 p_x\right), \pi^*\left(2 p_y\right)$$ all have one nodal plane each. The molecular orbitals formed by the combination of $$p$$ atomic orbitals in a sideways manner are indeed termed as $$\pi$$ and $$\pi^*$$ orbitals. Specifically, $$\pi$$ orbitals are bonding molecular orbitals with no nodal planes through the bonding region, whereas $$\pi^*$$ orbitals are antibonding molecular orbitals with one nodal plane through the bonding region. Thus, $$\pi$$ orbitals should ideally have no nodal planes through the bonding region, and $$\pi^*$$ orbitals should have one. Therefore, the assertion that all the mentioned orbitals, including the $$\pi$$ orbitals, have one nodal plane each is incorrect.

Analysis of Reason (R):

The reason given states that all molecular orbitals formed by the sideways overlapping have one nodal plane. This is a generalized statement that is not fully accurate. The $$\pi$$ molecular orbitals, formed by sideways overlap of unhybridized $$p$$ orbitals, typically have no nodal planes through the bonding region (areas between the nuclei involved in bonding), whereas $$\pi^*$$ orbitals, which are antibonding, do have such a nodal plane. Thus, the reason is overly generalized and misleading.

Conclusion:

Given the incorrect statement in both the assertion and the reason, and the incorrect association between them, the correct option is:

Option B: Both assertion and reason are false.

content:
Arrange the following in the decreasing order of their Dipole moments.

a. Chlorobenzene

b. 1,2-Dichlorobenzene

Which of the following molecules does not exhibit dipole moment?

(i) CCl$$_4$$ (ii) CO$$_2$$ (iii) NH$$_3$$ (iv) CHCl$$_3$$ (v) H$$_2$$O (vi) CH$$_3$$$$-$$O$$-$$CH$$_3$$
explanation: Dipole moments cancel out reach other in CCl$$_4$$ and CO$$_2$$ resulting in net dipole moment as zero because these are symmetrical structures.

content: Increasing order of bond order of oxygen and its ions is
explanation:
According to MOT, bond order is given by the formula, $$BO = {{{N_b} - {N_a}} \over 2}$$ where $$N_a,N_b$$ are the number of electrons in antibonding and bonding molecular orbital respectively.

Bond order for $${O_2} = {{10 - 6} \over 2} = 2$$

$$O_2^ - = {{10 - 7} \over 2} = 1.5$$

$$O_2^ + = {{10 - 5} \over 2} = 2.5$$

$$O_2^{2 - } = {{10 - 8} \over 2} = 1$$

content: The P-O bond order in PO$$_4^{3-}$$ is
explanation:

$$=\frac{5}{4}=1.25$$

content: Which of the following compound does not exists?

content: Find the correct order of C$$-$$O bond length among CO, CO$$_3^{2-}$$, CO$$_2$$.
explanation:
A multiple bond is always shorter bond the corresponding single bond. The C-atom in CO$$_3^{2-}$$ is sp$$^2$$-hybridised.

CO$$_2$$ is sp-hybridised with bond length of carbon oxygen is 122 pm.

The C-atom in CO is sp-hybridised with C$$-$$O bond length 110 pm.

$$^+$$C $$\equiv$$ O$$^{-}$$

So, the correct order of bond length is

CO < CO$$_2$$ < CO$$_3^{2-}$$.

content: Which of the following pairs of ions in iso-electronic and iso-structural?
explanation:
In ClO$$_3^{-}$$, total number of electrons = 42

In CO$$_3^{2-}$$, total number of electrons = 32

In NO$$_3^{-}$$, total number of electrons = 32

In SO$$_3^{2-}$$, total number of electrons = 42

So, the option (a) is correct.

content: During the formation of a chemical bond

explanation: During the formation of a chemical bond energy of system decreases with the release of energy. This result in stability of bond formed.

content: For a stable molecule the value of bond order must be

options.content: there is no relationship between stability and bond order

explanation:
For a stable molecule the value of bond order must be positive.

$$\because$$ Stability is directly proportional to bond order.

content:
The number of antibonding electron pairs in O$$_2^{2-}$$ molecular ion on the basis of molecular orbital theory is

[Atomic number of O is 18]
explanation:
Configuration of $$\mathrm{O}_2^{2-}=\sigma 1 s^2, \sigma^* 1 s^2, \sigma 2 s{ }^2, \sigma^* 2 s^2, \sigma 2 p_z^2, \pi 2 p_x^2 \approx \pi 2 p_y^2, \pi^* 2 p_x^2 \approx \pi^* 2 p_y^2$$.

$$\therefore$$ Number of antibonding electron pairs is four.
